title: Consolidating a loose text block, book repair for detached signatures
subtitle: How to stabilise pages that have pulled free from the binding
excerpt: When signatures come loose from a book's spine, the pages fan out and the structure collapses. Consolidation tightens the text block without full rebinding, the right repair for books with good boards and a damaged or missing spine lining.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on the care and repair of books: the consolidation of detached signatures using paste or methylcellulose, with guidance on pressing the repaired spine. The technique described here follows Cockerell's conservative approach of using reversible adhesives on the spine folds.

glossaryTerms.definition: The folded edge of a signature, the edge that faces the binding's spine.
An adhesive that can be re-softened with water, allowing a repair to be undone without damaging the original material. Wheat-paste and methylcellulose are both reversible; PVA is not.

content.text: Use this repair when the signatures are intact but their
have pulled apart from the spine lining. The goal is to rejoin them with a thin, flexible

attrs.title: Assess before you start
attrs.body: Check the sewing before applying any adhesive. If the thread is broken or missing, consolidation alone won't hold, the book needs re-sewing. Consolidation is for books where the sewing thread is intact but the spine adhesive has failed or dried out.

items.name: Methylcellulose
items.notes: Mixed to a thin paste consistency. Reversible, slow-tack, acid-free. Preferred for paper objects because it doesn't stain and remains re-soluable.

items.name: Wheat-paste (cooked)
items.notes: An alternative to methylcellulose, slightly stronger. Both are reversible, the correct choice for archival or historically significant books.

items.name: Soft brush (conservation)
items.notes: A wide, soft-bristled brush for applying paste between signature folds.

content.text: Remove the book from its boards if they are still attached, opening the boards with force will worsen the damage to the spine folds. If the boards are firmly attached, work with the book open flat. Fan the loose signatures slightly to expose the spine folds.

Load the soft brush with a thin coat of methylcellulose. Slide the brush between each pair of adjacent signature spine folds, applying a thin layer to both facing surfaces. Work from the head of the book to the tail. Do not apply adhesive to the face of the spine, only to the joins between signatures.

Close the book gently. Tap the head and tail edges on the table to square the signatures. Wrap a strip of scrap paper around the spine and press the book between boards under weight for at least four hours, preferably overnight. The paste needs time to dry fully before the spine is loaded.

items.symptom: Signatures still fan out after pressing
items.cause: The adhesive was too thin or too little was applied.
items.fix: Open the spine, add a thicker layer of methylcellulose between each loose fold, close the book, and press again.

items.symptom: Pages are stuck together after pressing
items.cause: Adhesive spread onto the page surfaces instead of staying between the folds.
items.fix: Use a finer brush and apply less adhesive. A small amount between the folds is enough, excess is what causes pages to stick.
